title: Li Bai
summary: Li Bai (701–762), also written Li Po, is a poet of the Chinese Tang dynasty, honored since his own century as the shixian, the Immortal of Poetry. Roughly a thousand poems survive under his name, built from wine, moonlight, mountain travel, parting friends, and a Daoist appetite for freedom. He is conventionally paired with his friend Du Fu as one of the two greatest poets in the Chinese tradition.
hook: In one of the most quoted poems in Chinese, a man sets a jug among the flowers with nobody to drink with, so he invites the moon, and the moon brings his shadow, and the three of them make a party. He admits the moon cannot drink and the shadow only copies him. He toasts them anyway. It is a poem about being completely alone that somehow reads like the best night of your life.
associatedWorks: The Road to Shu Is Hard

beginner.theFigure:
At about twenty-four, Li Bai walked out of Sichuan with a sword and a purse, and he never really settled again. He gave his family's money away to friends met on the road. He climbed mountains, drank in wine shops, and wrote poems for people he would never see twice.

He was born in 701, when the Tang dynasty was rich and sure of itself. Nearly every educated man of his time wanted a government post, and studied for years to sit a hard state exam. Li Bai never sat it. His murky family background may have blocked him, and he seems to have liked standing outside.

So he made his name another way. Travelers carried his poems across the country, and by his late thirties he was famous. In 742 the emperor Xuanzong called him to the capital, Chang'an, on that reputation alone, and he took a court post writing verses to order for the palace.

It did not last. After about two years he was gone, with gifts of gold and nowhere in particular to be. He spent the rest of his life traveling, drinking, writing, and hoping for a patron who never arrived.
beginner.bodyOfWork:
Roughly a thousand poems have come down to us under his name, and some may be by other hands. He never gathered his work into a book. He wrote for a moment, gave the paper away, and left later readers to collect the rest.

Three things fill those poems: wine, the moon, and the road. He wrote about drinking with friends, about goodbyes at a river crossing, and about waterfalls, cliff paths, and the huge empty sky.

His best loved poem is very short. In 'Quiet Night Thought' a man wakes at night, sees moonlight on the floor, mistakes it for frost, looks up at the moon, and thinks of home. Chinese children still learn it as their first poem.

Another famous one is 'Bring in the Wine,' which rolls forward like a shout. The river runs to the sea and never comes back. Hair turns white in a single morning. So fill the cup and do not stop. It is a poem about death that sounds like a party.

Li Bai often chose older, looser forms rather than the strict ones his age prized. His lines move fast, jump between pictures, and feel spoken rather than built.
beginner.howBeenRead:
People treated Li Bai as a visitor from somewhere else. An older official, He Zhizhang, called him a banished immortal, a spirit thrown out of heaven for some mistake. The name stuck, and later ages gave him a title of his own, the Immortal of Poetry.

Some of that came from his beliefs. Li Bai was drawn to Daoism, a tradition that prizes nature, ease, and letting go of ambition, and he was ordained a Daoist after leaving the court. His poems are full of clouds, flights, and mountains where an ordinary man turns into something lighter than air.

Then came the war. In 755 the general An Lushan rebelled and the empire nearly came apart. He joined the staff of a prince named Yong, who was soon accused of seizing power for himself. Li Bai was arrested and sentenced to exile in Yelang, far southwest, but a pardon reached him on the road in 759.

He died in 762 at a kinsman's house in Dangtu. An old story says instead that he drowned while drunk, leaning from a boat to embrace the moon's reflection. Scholars do not believe it. Everyone repeats it anyway, because it sounds so much like him.
beginner.whyItEndured:
Li Bai lasts because he is easy to love and hard to copy. His poems look simple: a cup, a road, a mountain, a friend leaving. A reader who knows nothing of the Tang dynasty can feel them straight away.

He also gave China a picture of freedom. Most poets of his day wrote as officials, careful about duty and rank. Li Bai wrote as a man who refused to be held. He praised wine, mocked ceremony, and treated the empire as a place to wander through. That figure thrilled readers then, and it still does.

His friend Du Fu is the other giant of Tang poetry, the serious one, the poet of war and duty. The two met in 744 and traveled together for a season, and Du Fu wrote several poems about him afterward. Chinese readers have paired them ever since.

There is one more reason. Li Bai made the natural world feel like company: a mountain he stares at until neither tires, a moon that follows him down a road, a night that stops feeling empty. He turned being alone into something a reader might want.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
The trouble with loving Li Bai is that the legend keeps eating the man. Stories grew fast. He wrote drunk, he never needed a second draft, and he once made a palace eunuch kneel and pull off his boots. Most of these tales come from long after his death, and they make his work look effortless.

It was not effortless. Li Bai knew the older poetry deeply and reshaped it with skill, so calling him a pure natural talent hides the labor inside the lines.

He has also been put to use. In 1971 a leading Chinese writer published a book praising Li Bai and running Du Fu down, and many readers took it as politics dressed as criticism. Each age seems to want one of the two friends more.

Abroad he arrived by a strange route. In 1915 the American poet Ezra Pound published Cathay, a slim book of Chinese poems in English, mostly Li Bai's. Pound could not read Chinese. He worked from a dead scholar's notebooks and printed the poet's name in its Japanese form. The versions are loose and beautiful. They reshaped English poetry, and they made Li Bai a world figure through a translator who could not read him.

general.theFigure:
Li Bai reached the Tang capital in 742 not as a candidate for office but as a celebrity, summoned by the emperor Xuanzong on the strength of poems that admirers had been copying for years. The senior official He Zhizhang, meeting him, is said to have named him a banished immortal, a phrase meaning a spirit exiled from heaven into ordinary human company. It was flattery, but it caught something real about the impression he made.

The court did not keep him. He was gone within roughly two years, having spent the interval composing ceremonial verses on demand for palace occasions. He left with gifts of gold and returned to the traveling life he had adopted near his twenty-fourth year. He had never sat the civil service examinations that organized virtually every literary career of his generation. His origins are genuinely obscure, and one influential reconstruction places his birth far out on the Central Asian frontier, probably in or near Suyab, before the family resettled in Sichuan. Perhaps those obscure and possibly commercial origins disqualified him. Perhaps he simply declined the ordeal. Either way the consequence was the same: his reputation was built outside the institution that built everyone else's.

That anomaly explains him better than temperament does. A Tang poet ordinarily wrote as an official or as an applicant for office, and obligation runs through the resulting verse like a watermark. Li Bai wrote instead as a guest who might leave at any hour, courting patrons and craving recognition, yet addressing the empire as a landscape to cross rather than a hierarchy to climb.
general.bodyOfWork:
About a thousand poems survive under his name, and the figure is deliberately soft. Li Bai assembled no collection of his own, preferring to write for an occasion and hand the sheet to whoever was standing there, so his work had to be gathered after his death from scattered copies. Some poems in the received body are probably not his, and an unknown quantity of genuine work has certainly vanished.

What survives falls into three broad moods. There is the drinking poetry, of which 'Bring in the Wine' is the most extravagant: the river runs to the sea and never returns, black hair whitens by evening, and the cup should never be allowed to stand empty. There is the travel poetry, including 'The Road to Shu Is Hard,' which climbs a mountain track in lines that lurch and break to imitate the ascent. And there is the quiet poetry of distance and homesickness, whose purest example is 'Quiet Night Thought,' four lines that remain the first poem most Chinese children commit to memory.

His technical decisions carry an argument. The prestige form of the age was regulated verse, governed by fixed length, strict tonal patterns, and matched couplets. Li Bai could manage it perfectly well, but he preferred older ballad measures and free-running songs that let a line stretch or snap, producing an effect of speech rather than construction. Later readers mistook that effect for proof he never labored, though his poems are thickly woven with borrowed phrases and echoes of earlier verse, reworked with obvious deliberation.
general.howBeenRead:
Almost from the beginning, readers accounted for Li Bai by suggesting he was not entirely mortal. The banished-immortal label was merely the earliest version of that instinct. By later centuries he carried a formal title, shixian, the Immortal of Poetry, positioned against the title awarded to Du Fu, the Sage of Poetry. One term points upward and away from the world. The other points to responsibility inside it.

Daoism supplied that reading with genuine material, and Li Bai accepted a formal Daoist ordination after leaving the court. His poems are crowded with the tradition's imagery of cranes, cloud-riding, elixirs of long life, and mountains where a man sheds his heaviness. Whether this represented conviction, temperament, or a magnificent supply of metaphor has never been settled, and the poems decline to force the choice.

The rebellion that began in 755 tested the pose and destroyed his position. Li Bai joined the staff of Prince Yong, a son of the emperor, during the confused factional struggle that followed, and when the prince was eliminated as a rival claimant Li Bai was arrested and condemned. How far he grasped the prince's ambitions, or shared them, is disputed rather than settled. His sentence was commuted to banishment at Yelang, a remote posting in the southwest, and an amnesty overtook him on the journey in 759. The end came in 762 under a kinsman's roof at Dangtu. The celebrated alternative account, that he drowned while drunk reaching for the moon's reflection, is a later legend rather than a record, and it survives because it matches the man his readers had already decided he was.
general.whyItEndured:
The first reason is accessibility. Li Bai's images are common property: a cup, a moon, a mountain, a friend on a departing boat. A reader with no knowledge of the eighth century can enter the poem on its opening line, which is not true of most classical Chinese verse. The plainness explains a great deal about why his work travels and why it anchors school curricula.

The second reason is the kind of person the poems invent. Tang literary culture was organized around service, examination, and rank, and its poetry carries that weight everywhere. Li Bai offered an alternative self, unbought and unimpressed, at home in wine and open country, treating the empire as scenery rather than as an employer. The figure was partly a performance, since he pursued patronage his entire life, but it still worked as a genuine release for readers bound to their obligations, and it works that way now.

The third reason is the pairing with Du Fu. The two poets met in 744, traveled together briefly, and Du Fu wrote about him repeatedly afterward. Chinese criticism has held them together ever since as opposite poles of a single golden age. The Daoist wanderer stands against the Confucian witness, ecstasy against conscience. Each is sharpened by the other, and the pair became a convenient way of arguing about what poetry is for. A tradition that preserved both preserved two answers instead of settling on one.
general.contestedInheritance:
The central quarrel over Li Bai is between the legend and the craftsman. Anecdote made him a drunken genius who never revised, who composed at imperial command while barely conscious, and who forced a powerful eunuch to remove his boots. Most of that material appears well after his lifetime, and it flatters and insults him at the same time, since a poet who merely spills verse is a natural phenomenon rather than an artist. Close reading produces a different figure entirely: a writer saturated in earlier poetry, reworking inherited phrases with precision and choosing his forms for their looseness. The spontaneity is an achieved effect rather than an absence of effort.

Politics has pulled at him as well. Because the pairing with Du Fu encodes a judgment about freedom against duty, promoting one poet has usually required demoting the other. In 1971 the writer Guo Moruo published a book elevating Li Bai and disparaging Du Fu, widely understood as a bid for official favor during the Cultural Revolution rather than as literary argument. The episode shows how quickly a poet twelve centuries dead becomes a counter in a living dispute.

His foreign career raises a different difficulty. Ezra Pound's Cathay, published in 1915, carried mostly Li Bai poems into English and helped reshape modern verse. Pound knew no Chinese, and he worked from the notebooks of the late scholar Ernest Fenollosa, printing the poet's name in its Japanese form. The resulting poems are superb and frequently unfaithful, which means his global fame rests substantially on beautiful approximations. That leaves an uncomfortable question with no tidy answer: how much of a poet can survive a translator who cannot read him?

advanced.theFigure:
The most consequential fact about Li Bai is not any event in his biography but the extraordinary permeability of the corpus that carries his name. He assembled no collection, wrote occasionally and disposed of the paper, and left a body of work reconstructed posthumously from copies, anthologies, and household memory. The roughly one thousand poems attributed to him are therefore a reception artifact as much as an authorial one, and the attribution boundary is porous in a direction that is not random: poems that sound sufficiently like Li Bai have had a standing invitation into the corpus for twelve centuries. The persona is thus partly self-generating, since each accretion reinforces the template by which the next accretion is judged.

This has an uncomfortable consequence for the biographical reading that dominates popular accounts. The wandering drinker who scorned office is reconstructed principally from the poems, and the poems have been selected and stabilized by readers already committed to that figure. Paula Varsano's study of his critical reception makes the circularity visible by treating the interpretive vocabulary itself as the object: the language of naturalness, spontaneity, and transcendence developed over centuries to account for Li Bai, and then determined what would count as authentically his. The result is not that the received Li Bai is false. It is that the evidence and the interpretation are entangled at a depth that resists the usual separation of poet from myth, and any confident biography must state where it has cut that knot.
advanced.bodyOfWork:
The reflex that treats Li Bai's manner as unmediated utterance survives because it is flattering and because it is hard to falsify from translation. The corrective is philological and it is decisive. His diction is saturated with the earlier tradition: yuefu ballad frames, Chuci flight imagery, Daoist technical vocabulary of transcendence with a precise doctrinal register behind it. What reads as pure impulse is very often an inherited formula placed under new pressure. The apparent artlessness is an achieved register, and achieving it in a literary culture organized around the display of parallelism and tonal control was a considerable act of positioning.

That positioning is the real formal argument of the work. By favoring old-style verse and song forms over regulated verse at the moment when regulated verse carried the highest prestige, Li Bai converted a technical preference into a claim about the poet's relation to institutions. The loose line is not merely a stylistic taste; it enacts a refusal of the disciplined, symmetrical, examination-adjacent aesthetic that governed elite composition. Read this way, the drinking poems stop being autobiography and become polemic. 'Bring in the Wine' does not report a night of excess so much as stage an argument against the whole calculus of accumulation, office, and posthumous reputation that organized the lives of his readers. Its extravagance is a position, and its speaker is a constructed instrument for holding that position, not a transcript of the man.
advanced.howBeenRead:
The Li Bai and Du Fu binary is usually presented as an observation about two poets. It is better understood as a critical instrument that the tradition built and then required both poets to fit. The paired honorifics, Immortal against Sage, are not descriptions arrived at independently; they are complementary terms in a single scheme that allocates the whole territory of poetic value between transcendence and moral responsibility. Once the scheme existed, everything either poet wrote could be read as evidence for his assigned pole, and the substantial overlap between them, Li Bai's poems of political grievance, Du Fu's poems of drunken release, became noise rather than data.

The stakes of the scheme are visible in the way preference tracks power. Song critics who elevated Du Fu were arguing for an ethics of engaged service; twentieth-century promotions of Li Bai over Du Fu, most nakedly in 1971, were arguing about class origin and about whose favor the argument sought. In each case the ranking is a proxy. What makes this awkward rather than merely interesting is that no reader now has independent access to a pre-scheme Li Bai. The transmitted corpus was shaped by the same tradition that built the binary, so the poet available for comparison has already been selected to make the comparison work. Detecting the distortion is straightforward; getting behind it is not, and claims to have done so should be treated as claims about method rather than discoveries.
advanced.whyItEndured:
The standard explanation of Li Bai's durability, that his images are universal and his feeling immediate, is doing less work than it appears. Immediacy is a property of a reading, not of a text, and the immediacy attributed to Li Bai is produced by a specific chain of mediations: a stabilized school canon, a small set of anthology pieces, and translations that systematically favor the transparent poem over the allusive one. 'Quiet Night Thought' is universal partly because centuries of selection made it the entry point, and its four lines exclude almost everything difficult in the corpus. Endurance of that kind is curatorial before it is aesthetic.

The more interesting mechanism is that Li Bai occupies a slot the tradition could not otherwise fill. A literary culture whose central institution was the examination and whose dominant ethic was service required an authorized figure of refusal, someone through whom the unlived alternative could be enjoyed without being adopted. Li Bai supplies it, and the supply is safest precisely because he failed. He got two years at court, lost them, backed a losing prince, and was exiled; the freedom he modeled cost him everything the system could withhold. That failure is what makes the figure usable, since a Li Bai who had won would have been a threat to the order rather than its licensed release valve. His endurance is the endurance of a permitted exception, and the permission is what needs explaining, not the charm.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The sharpest problem in Li Bai's afterlife is that his most consequential Western transmission is also his least reliable one, and the two facts cannot be separated. Pound's Cathay in 1915 worked from Ernest Fenollosa's notebooks, mediated through Japanese readings, by a poet with no Chinese, and it produced English poems of such authority that they altered the cadence of modern verse. To call them mistranslations is accurate and useless. They function as originals in the receiving tradition, and Li Bai's global standing descends from them rather than from any philologically defensible version. The awkward implication is that the fidelity criterion, which scholarship must uphold, does not explain the transmission that actually happened.

The temptation is to resolve this by ranking: the real Li Bai in Chinese, the useful fiction in English. That resolution is too clean, because the Chinese Li Bai is himself a curated and partly apocryphal construction, refined by the same appetite for a legible persona that Pound served. The honest position holds that there is no unmediated poet to appeal to as a standard, only a sequence of interested reconstructions of differing evidentiary quality, and that the difference between the Song anthologist's Li Bai and Pound's is one of degree and distance rather than of kind. What follows is a demand for specificity rather than despair. Every claim about Li Bai should carry the reconstruction it depends on, name the selection that produced its evidence, and stop short of the man, who is not recoverable behind the apparatus that preserved him.

foundation.theStory: Li Bai is the case study for a poet who became a personality. Born in 701, he skipped the examination route that structured Tang literary careers, made his name by circulation rather than office, spent about two years at Emperor Xuanzong's court from 742, and lived the rest of his life traveling. The An Lushan Rebellion caught him on the wrong side: he served Prince Yong, was arrested, was sentenced to exile in Yelang, and was pardoned in 759. He died in 762. Around a thousand poems survive under his name, assembled after his death, and the figure they project, free, drunk, half-divine, has proved more durable than any fact about him.
foundation.theDebate: Three arguments run through the study of Li Bai. The first concerns spontaneity: the tradition insists he wrote without effort, while philological work shows dense reworking of earlier poetry, so the question is whether the ease is a fact about composition or an effect he engineered. The second concerns the corpus: because the poems were gathered posthumously from scattered copies, some attributions are doubtful, and scholars disagree about how much of the received body is securely his. The third concerns the pairing with Du Fu, which some treat as an accurate description of two temperaments and others as a critical scheme that shaped how both poets were read and even which of their poems survived in prominence.
foundation.whyItMatters: Working through Li Bai opens the Tang dynasty at its height and at its breaking point, the examination culture that organized elite Chinese life, and Daoism as both a religion and a supply of literary imagery. It also opens two questions with reach beyond China: how a reputation can outgrow the evidence that supports it, and what happens when a body of work enters world literature through translations made by someone who cannot read the original.
questions: Li Bai never collected his own poems, and later readers gathered them from copies. What problems does that create for anyone who wants to know what he actually wrote?
Chinese tradition pairs Li Bai the free spirit with Du Fu the dutiful witness. What does a culture gain by keeping two opposite models of the poet instead of choosing one?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: English-language study of Li Bai has a thin but pointed shelf. Arthur Waley's The Poetry and Career of Li Po (1950) supplied the first substantial biography and set a deflationary tone, treating the legends skeptically and the poems as documents. Paula Varsano's Tracking the Banished Immortal (2003) reoriented the field by making the critical language itself the subject, showing how centuries of readers built a vocabulary of naturalness and transcendence that then governed what counted as authentically Li Bai. Stephen Owen situates him within the High Tang rather than above it, which has the effect of restoring his competitors and his context. Ha Jin's biography brought the life to a general readership. The main fault line runs between reading the poems for the man and reading the reception for the figure.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is lopsided. The poems are abundant and richly studied, and the public events they touch, the court summons, the rebellion, the exile and amnesty, are corroborated by Tang records. What is thin is independent testimony about the private man. Nearly everything colorful in the standard portrait, the drunken composition, the boot-removing eunuch, the drowning, comes from anecdote collections written after his death, and the drowning story has no support at all. Attribution is a further limit: the corpus was assembled posthumously and some poems in it are probably not his. Any claim about Li Bai's temperament, motives, or beliefs rests on a persona built inside the poems, and the poems were preserved by readers who already loved that persona.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible position must decline both the legend and the flat debunking. Take 'Bring in the Wine.' The legend reads it as a transcript of a great night; the debunker reads it as a conventional exercise in a yuefu frame. Both are weaker than the poem. Its power comes from placing an inherited form under real pressure, so that a familiar drinking-song structure is made to carry an argument against accumulation, office, and the pursuit of a name that outlives you. That argument is aimed precisely at the readers whose lives were organized by those goals. Reading it that way requires holding two things at once: that the ease is manufactured, and that what the manufactured ease says is meant seriously. A strong argument about Li Bai shows a specific poem doing that, rather than deciding in advance whether he was sincere.
questions: Almost every vivid detail in the popular image of Li Bai comes from anecdotes written after his death. What can such sources legitimately establish, and at what point must a careful reader set them aside?
Ezra Pound's versions made Li Bai a world poet, and Pound could not read Chinese. If a translation without linguistic fidelity produces the reception that matters, what should scholarship do with it?

research.topic: Li Bai's life, corpus, and standing
research.content: It is well established that Li Bai lived from 701 to 762, that he did not pursue the imperial examinations, and that he was summoned to Emperor Xuanzong's court at Chang'an around 742 and left it after roughly two years. It is equally well established that he served on the staff of Prince Yong during the upheaval following the An Lushan Rebellion of 755, was arrested when the prince was destroyed, was sentenced to exile in Yelang, and received an amnesty in 759 before reaching it. He died in 762 at Dangtu. Roughly a thousand poems survive under his name, and he has been honored since medieval times as the shixian, the Immortal of Poetry, and paired with Du Fu, whom he met in 744.

research.topic: Reception and persona as the object of study
research.content: A significant strand of recent scholarship studies not Li Bai but the critical machinery that produced him. This work traces how readers from the ninth century onward developed a specialized vocabulary of naturalness, spontaneity, and transcendence to account for his poetry, and how that vocabulary then shaped which poems were preserved, anthologized, and accepted as authentically his. It treats the pairing with Du Fu as an interpretive scheme rather than a neutral observation, and it examines the Western reception, above all Ezra Pound's Cathay of 1915, as a further act of construction rather than a transmission. Associated in English with Paula Varsano and with the contextual approach of Stephen Owen, this remains an active area rather than a settled one.

research.topic: The man behind the persona, and the limits of the corpus
research.content: What cannot be recovered is the private Li Bai and the exact shape of what he wrote. Resolving either would require contemporary documentation of his household, movements, and habits, and manuscript evidence of his own arrangement of his poems. Neither exists: he kept no collection, and the surviving corpus was assembled after his death from scattered copies and anthologies, so a portion of it is of doubtful attribution while an unknown quantity of genuine work is lost. The vivid anecdotes that carry the popular image, composition while drunk, the humiliated eunuch, the drowning while embracing the moon's reflection, derive from later collections rather than contemporary record, and the drowning is legend. What can be responsibly inferred is the public outline, corroborated by Tang sources: the court episode, the rebellion, the exile, the pardon, the death at Dangtu. What must remain unknowable is how far the intoxicated, unbuyable speaker of the poems corresponds to the man who wrote them.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Immortal of Poetry
comparativeNarrative.content: The traditional Chinese view grants Li Bai a title that lifts him out of the human competition. He is the shixian, the Immortal of Poetry, set beside Du Fu, the Sage of Poetry. The terms are complementary rather than rival: one names transcendence, the other moral duty, and between them they cover what poetry can be for. This reading prizes his apparent ease, his refusal of the strict prestige forms, and the flights of Daoist imagery that make his speaker seem lighter than his readers. Its cost is that it explains everything by temperament. A poet declared naturally inspired can never be shown working, so the deliberate craft inside the loose line goes largely unexamined.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Free Spirit, Conscripted
comparativeNarrative.content: In modern China, Li Bai arrives first through school. His short poems on moonlight and homesickness are memorized by nearly every child, which makes him a shared possession before he is a subject of study. That familiarity has political uses. Because the pairing with Du Fu carries a judgment about freedom against duty, raising one poet has often meant lowering the other. In 1971 the writer Guo Moruo published a book that praised Li Bai and disparaged Du Fu, read at the time as a bid for official favor rather than as criticism. The wandering outsider who mocked rank can thus be recruited by the state that outranks everyone, and the classroom version quietly loses his failure and his exile.

comparativeNarrative.name: Rihaku, Invented in English
comparativeNarrative.content: The West met Li Bai through Ezra Pound's Cathay in 1915, a thin book of translations that changed the sound of English verse. Pound could not read Chinese. He worked from the notebooks of the scholar Ernest Fenollosa, who had studied the poems in Japan, and he printed the poet's name in its Japanese form. The results are magnificent and frequently unfaithful. This vantage reveals something the Chinese tradition cannot see from inside: that Li Bai's world fame rests on approximations, and that his plainest images survive translation while his allusions and sound do not. It also shows the limit of fidelity as a measure, since the inaccurate versions are the ones that carried him.

comparativeSummary: Set side by side, these vantage points show a poet whose fame is secure and whose substance keeps shifting under it. The classical tradition made him an immortal and thereby excused itself from studying his craft; modern China made him a schoolroom treasure and then a piece in an argument about duty; the West made him a world poet through translations that could not reach his language. Each account rests on something genuinely in the poems, and each depends on a selection someone else made. The disagreement is not about whether Li Bai was great but about how much of him any given reading has to leave out to be usable.

rabbitHole.label: Du Fu
rabbitHole.reason: Du Fu was Li Bai's friend and the other pole of Tang poetry, the moral witness against the Daoist wanderer. Chinese criticism has read them as a matched pair for a thousand years, and neither figure is fully legible without the other.

rabbitHole.label: The Tang Dynasty
rabbitHole.reason: Li Bai's career depends on the Tang at its most confident: a cosmopolitan capital, an examination culture he refused, and an imperial court that could summon a poet on reputation alone. The dynasty's near-collapse then set the terms of his ruin.

rabbitHole.label: Daoism
rabbitHole.reason: Li Bai took formal Daoist ordination and drew his imagery of flight, transcendence, and release from the tradition's stock. Whether it was belief or technique is unsettled, and the question cannot be posed without the tradition itself.

rabbitHole.label: The An Lushan Rebellion
rabbitHole.reason: The rebellion of 755 pulled Li Bai onto the staff of Prince Yong and ended with his arrest, condemnation, and sentence of exile to Yelang. The war turned a celebrated poet into a political liability in under two years.

rabbitHole.label: Confucianism
rabbitHole.reason: The Confucian ideal of the scholar in service is the order Li Bai's poems push against, and the honorific Sage given to Du Fu comes from it. Seeing what he refused clarifies what his refusal was worth to his readers.

rabbitHole.label: Chinese Calligraphy
rabbitHole.reason: Tang calligraphy prized the same paradox that governs Li Bai's reputation: a spontaneity that in fact requires immense training. The wild cursive scripts of his century are the visual version of the argument about his supposed effortlessness.

rabbitHole.label: Chinese Landscape Painting
rabbitHole.reason: The mountains, cataracts, and vanishing travelers of Li Bai's poems became a standing subject for Chinese painters, who worked the same Daoist sense of a human figure dwarfed and consoled by scale. The two arts read each other.

rabbitHole.label: Omar Khayyam
rabbitHole.reason: Another poet of wine, mortality, and refusal whose global fame arrives through a loose and beautiful translation rather than his own language. The parallel sharpens the question of what survives when a poet travels by approximation.

reference.author: Varsano, Paula M.
reference.title: Tracking the Banished Immortal: The Poetry of Li Bo and Its Critical Reception
reference.annotation: The first full-length English study of Li Bai in half a century, and the one that made his reception the object rather than the background. The entry draws on it for the argument that the vocabulary of naturalness and transcendence shaped which poems came to count as authentically his.

reference.author: Waley, Arthur
reference.title: The Poetry and Career of Li Po, 701-762 A.D.
reference.annotation: The first substantial biography in any language, and pointedly unsentimental about the anecdotes that had accumulated around the poet. The entry uses it for the shape of the career and for the deflationary handling of the drunken-genius material.

reference.author: Owen, Stephen
reference.title: The Great Age of Chinese Poetry: The High T'ang
reference.annotation: Places Li Bai inside the crowded poetic world of his century rather than on a pedestal above it. The entry relies on it for the competitive context, for the standing of regulated verse, and for weighing his formal choices as choices.

reference.author: Owen, Stephen (ed. and trans.)
reference.title: An Anthology of Chinese Literature: Beginnings to 1911
reference.annotation: A translated survey with commentary that shows the earlier forms and phrases Li Bai reworked. The entry uses it to test the claim of unmediated spontaneity against the inherited material visibly at work in the poems.

reference.author: Ha Jin
reference.title: The Banished Immortal: A Life of Li Bai (Li Po)
reference.annotation: A novelist's reconstruction of the life for general readers, careful to mark where the record stops and the legend begins. The entry uses it chiefly as a gauge of how the popular portrait is currently assembled in English.

reference.author: Pound, Ezra (trans.)
reference.title: Cathay
reference.annotation: Pound's slim volume, whose title page renders the poems 'for the most part from the Chinese of Rihaku,' carried mostly Li Bai into English from the notebooks of the late Ernest Fenollosa. The entry treats it as the primary artifact of the Western reception: a set of loose and frequently unfaithful versions made by a translator who knew no Chinese, and the vehicle through which Li Bai became a world figure.

commerce.author: Hinton, David (trans.)
commerce.title: The Selected Poems of Li Po
commerce.note: The version to open if you have never read him. Hinton keeps the density and the strangeness instead of smoothing them, so a first-time reader meets a poet rather than a museum label, and the selection covers the drinking, travel, and homesickness poems in one short volume.

commerce.author: Cooper, Arthur (trans.)
commerce.title: Li Po and Tu Fu
commerce.note: Because Cooper prints both poets between the same covers, with a long introduction on why they are always named together, the famous contrast stops being a slogan and becomes something you can test line by line against the poems themselves.

commerce.author: Ha Jin
commerce.title: The Banished Immortal: A Life of Li Bai (Li Po)
commerce.note: Pick this up if the poems make you want the life. A novelist tells it as a story while flagging where the evidence runs out, which is the right introduction for a reader who has heard the drunken-genius legend and wants to know how much of it holds.

commerce.author: Seaton, J. P. (trans.)
commerce.title: Bright Moon, White Clouds: Selected Poems of Li Po
commerce.note: Worth having once one translation is not enough. Seaton groups the poems by drinking, friendship, spirituality, protest, and travel, so the shape of a career emerges from the arrangement, and comparing his renderings against another translator's shows how much the English is a choice.

commerce.author: Owen, Stephen
commerce.title: The Great Age of Chinese Poetry: The High T'ang
commerce.note: Read this when you start wondering who Li Bai was competing with. Owen restores the crowded, ambitious literary world of the eighth century, which makes his refusal of the prestige forms look like a strategy rather than a temperament.

commerce.author: Varsano, Paula M.
commerce.title: Tracking the Banished Immortal: The Poetry of Li Bo and Its Critical Reception
commerce.note: The book for a reader ready to argue about him. Varsano tracks how twelve centuries of critics built the language of naturalness and transcendence, and how that language then decided what would count as a real Li Bai poem.

commerce.author: Waley, Arthur
commerce.title: The Poetry and Career of Li Po, 701-762 A.D.
commerce.note: Go here to watch a great translator refuse to be charmed. Waley wrote the first serious biography and treated the boot-removing eunuch and the drowned-embracing-the-moon stories as evidence about later readers, not about the poet.

popularCulture.title: Quiet Night Thought (c. 726, Li Bai)
popularCulture.description: Four lines: moonlight on the floor mistaken for frost, a lifted head, a thought of home. Chinese children learn it first, and most adults can still recite it. That makes Li Bai a shared possession before he is a subject. It also means many readers know only his simplest poem, and picture a gentle homesick man rather than a difficult one.

popularCulture.title: Chang An (2023, Xie Junwei and Zou Jing)
popularCulture.description: A long Chinese animated film that tells the Tang golden age through the friendship of Li Bai and the poet Gao Shi. It was a huge hit at home, and it puts the poems into people's mouths so viewers hear them spoken. The story is smoothed into a warm buddy tale, and Li Bai's political disgrace lands softer than it did.

popularCulture.title: Cathay (1915, Ezra Pound)
popularCulture.description: A thin book of Chinese poems in English, mostly Li Bai's, that changed how English poetry sounded. Pound could not read Chinese. He worked from a dead scholar's notes and used the Japanese form of the poet's name. The poems are lovely and often wrong, and much of the world still meets Li Bai through them.

popularCulture.title: Honor of Kings (2015, Tencent)
popularCulture.description: In one of the most played mobile games on earth, Li Bai is a fast sword-fighting hero who quotes poetry as he attacks. Millions of young players meet the name here first. The game keeps his wine and his swagger and drops everything else, so a poet of exile and failure becomes a stylish assassin with a good line.

popularCulture.title: The legend that Li Bai drowned embracing the moon
popularCulture.description: The best known story about his death says he leaned from a boat while drunk, reached for the moon's reflection, and fell in. There is no real evidence for it, and he most likely died at a relative's house in Dangtu in 762. The tale survives because it gives his life a perfect ending, which is exactly why it should be doubted.

popularCulture.title: The Tomb of Li Bai, Dangtu, Anhui
popularCulture.description: A walled grave at the foot of Qingshan Mountain, tended for centuries and now a protected national site with a park around it. Visitors come to honor a poet who spent his life refusing to settle anywhere. The calm grounds make a fitting shrine, though they smooth over the exile and the disgrace of his last years.
